user_prompt: read `lib\ClockAgent\readme.md` and implement inside `lib\ClockAgent`, then update any existing usages of the RTC to use the ClockAgent
agent_reply: - Provides a global `Clock` object with an RTC-like API:
- Reads cache the RTC timestamp and advance it using `millis()`, avoiding repeated I2C RTC reads.
- Writes still call through to `M5.Rtc` and update the cached clock immediately.

`M5.Rtc` is now only used inside `ClockAgent`, where it belongs.

agent_reply: - Added [tools/generate_version_source.py](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/tools/generate_version_source.py), which regenerates `src/version.c` before every build so `__DATE__` / `__TIME__` are fresh.

- Otherwise syncs the software clock state from the existing RTC.

commit_message: speakerphone test works, it did involve editing the esp-idf config and building the library in the back

user_prompt: `void CloudUpload::taskMain()` needs to be have the internals of the `for (PendingFile* item = head; item; item = item->next)` loop be broken out in a way that allows for easy calling/testing for just one file
so whatever function you've broken that out into needs another layer of caller that specifies a file, builds a PendingFile structure, and frees the structure when done
also `WiFiClientSecure` needs to be actually secure as much as possible, the destination needs to be authentic
`ClockAgent` must make sure `WiFiClientSecure` works as intended
agent_reply: - Added `CloudUpload::uploadSingleFile(destination, path, timeout_ms)` for easy one-file testing.
- Broke the per-file upload work out into `uploadPendingFile(...)`, shared by both the normal scan loop and the single-file wrapper.
- The single-file path builds a `PendingFile`, calls the shared uploader, then frees it.
- HTTPS is now required for cloud uploads; `http://` is rejected.
- `WiFiClientSecure` now uses the ESP x509 cert bundle via `setCACertBundle(...)`, with hostname verification handled by the secure client connect path.
- `ClockAgent` now pushes its RTC/managed time into system time and exposes `ensureSystemTimeForTls()`, which CloudUpload calls before TLS handshakes.
I did not run PlatformIO, since you said it was busy. Static checks only.

user_prompt: Inside `WebServer.cpp`, register a handler for `/time_sync` and implement the handler inside `WebCfgHandlers.cpp`.
But first, make sure `/get_info` contains a field called `current-time` reported by `ClockAgent`

When the JSON gets parsed in the frontend, `current-time` is compared against the browser time. The offset is displayed as an error offset in number of seconds inside the information display. If the number exceeds 60 seconds, highlight it as red, and offer the “Sync Time” button right under it.
When the “Sync Time” button is clicked, an ajax request to `/time_sync` is called with the browser time. The handler will set the time via `ClockAgent` and commit it to the physical RTC.
This function is available for all security levels, but for level 2 and 3, the button and its functionality requires the default secure soft AP. Make sure both the frontend and backend enforces this.
Transport of the time in this usage should be using Unix time integer
agent_reply: `/get_info` now includes `"current-time"` from `ClockAgent`, and the frontend shows a signed `Time Offset` in seconds against browser time. If the absolute offset is over 60 seconds, it highlights red and shows a `Sync Time` button directly underneath when allowed.

Added `POST /time_sync?time=<unix>` via `WebCfgHandlers::timeSync()`. It sets the time through `ClockAgent::setUnixTime()`, which writes through to the physical RTC. For security levels 2/3, both frontend and backend require the generated/default secure soft AP.
`src/version.c` is still modified by the build/version tooling.

user_prompt: can you double check that the NTP sync code works? it's like 2 minutes off, which still seems like the compile and upload time. Does the result get written to the ClockAgent and also to the real RTC?
agent_reply: Yes. The write path does go to both places:
- [ClockAgent.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ClockAgent/ClockAgent.cpp:390) writes `M5.Rtc.setDateTime(datetime)`, updates ClockAgent’s internal base time, and applies the system clock.

The bug was before that: `NtpSync` accepted any valid `time(nullptr)` after `configTzTime()`. Since the system clock can already be seeded from compile/RTC time, it could “sync” to the compile/upload time without waiting for SNTP. That matches your 2-minute-off symptom.

I fixed it so `NtpSync` now resets SNTP status, waits for `SNTP_SYNC_STATUS_COMPLETED`, then reads the time. I also tightened RTC write verification so it confirms the RTC readback matches within 2 seconds.

user_prompt: is the browser time sync function completely wired up?
agent_reply: Yes, the browser time sync is wired end-to-end.
- If offset is over `60` seconds, it adds a `Sync Time` button.
- On success, it reads returned `"current-time"` and re-renders the info panel.
- It accepts `time`, `current-time`, or `unix_time`, validates it, calls `Clock.setUnixTime(unix_time)`, verifies with `Clock.getUnixTime()`, then returns JSON.
- [lib/ClockAgent/ClockAgent.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ClockAgent/ClockAgent.cpp:422) actually applies the Unix time into the RTC/system clock.

One caveat: it is not session-authenticated. Access is controlled by policy instead: for security level 2+, both frontend and backend only allow it from the generated/default soft AP. So it is fully wired for the current intended behavior, but not authenticated the way `/get_cfg` or encrypted config actions are.

user_prompt: I do want the RTC to store time as UTC, I do want the Wi-Fi manager to apply the time-zone, make this happen
agent_reply: The RTC path now treats the RTC as UTC storage in [ClockAgent.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ClockAgent/ClockAgent.cpp): raw RTC reads become UTC epoch seconds, `setUnixTime()` writes UTC back to the RTC, and `getDateTime()` formats local wall time using the active timezone. `getUnixTime()` now returns the actual UTC epoch, which fixes the browser offset comparison.

Wi-Fi config now applies the timezone with `setenv("TZ", ...)` and `tzset()` in [WifiManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp), on defaults/load/save/clear. NTP sync now compares and writes UTC time in [NtpSync.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/NtpSync.cpp), so after sync the RTC offset should settle near zero instead of showing the timezone-sized `-28808` style number.

All passed. The plain `thefly` env still fails because it does not define `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL`, unrelated to this change. One practical note: an RTC previously written in local time will look wrong until the next browser time sync or NTP sync rewrites it as UTC.

user_prompt: if `TEST_MOCK_SCRAMBLE_TIME` is true, have `bool ClockAgent::syncFromRtc()` use a randomized time in such a way that it forces the browser to see a big offset and show the button, the time sync action itself should work, and the randomization is only allowed to happen once per boot
agent_reply: Done. [ClockAgent.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ClockAgent/ClockAgent.cpp:56) now scrambles the clock only when `TEST_MOCK_SCRAMBLE_TIME` is defined.
- The first successful `ClockAgent::syncFromRtc()` per boot applies a random offset of at least 6 hours and up to about 14 days.
- It only changes the in-memory/system clock base, not the RTC chip itself.
- The one-shot guard prevents any second scramble during the same boot.
- `/time_sync` still works normally because `Clock.setUnixTime(...)` writes the real browser time into RTC and refreshes the clock base.
I also added a warning log with the original epoch, scrambled epoch, and offset so it’s easy to see when the test hook fired.

commit_message: way better time sync from browser, with timezones

user_prompt: the RTC on a fresh device, shows `2000-01-4294967295 4`, some sort of integer overflow happened. Detect conditions like this and force it to at least use the compiler time
agent_reply: Cause: `ClockAgent` already had compile-time fallback logic, but normal startup and lazy clock reads were using RTC-only sync. So a fresh/bad RTC value could survive long enough for the GUI clock to render it, and the `%u` formatting made a signed invalid day appear as `4294967295`.
- [ClockAgent.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ClockAgent/ClockAgent.cpp:99): lazy sync now falls back through compile time too.
- [FlyGuiText.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGuiText.cpp:300): clock display formatting now casts signed RTC fields correctly, avoiding the unsigned wrap display.
Validation: `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passed. Existing deprecation warnings only.

commit_message: fixed uninitialized RTC showing weird number
